narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shown a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70, which stands out well above the typical 3.21 average among students in the dataset used for this prediction. However, your application was rejected because the model, trained on past students' data to forecast first-time bar exam success, estimated a 47% probability that you would fail the bar—crossing the threshold that led admissions to deny you. This prediction hinges on a few key aspects of your profile that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest positive factor in your favor was your undergraduate GPA; it significantly pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, reflecting how strong prior academic performance often correlates with success in law. Yet, this was outweighed by your LSAT score of 27, which is notably below the average of 36.70 and pushed the model toward predicting failure, as lower scores have historically been a hurdle for many students. Adding to that concern was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, well below the average of 5.53, which further nudged the prediction in the direction of bar exam struggles compared to peers who ranked higher. Your middle family income bracket and full-time student status aligned closely with the majority of applicants, so they didn't sway things much either way. Overall, while your GPA gives you a real strength, the gaps in your LSAT and later-year school performance made you appear riskier than typical admitted students, who tend to balance stronger test scores with consistent rankings. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. You might also reflect on what impacted your Year 3 decile—targeted study habits or tutoring could help replicate your undergrad success there. Building a narrative around your resilience as a Hispanic male pursuing law could strengthen supplemental essays for other schools. Many students in similar positions have improved outcomes by addressing these exact areas, so persistence will pay off. Keep aiming high; your GPA shows you have the foundation to succeed.
